input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system is developed within a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) framework, employing SysML v1.7 to capture requirements, functional, logical, and product layers. Component‑connector architecture is expressed using ADLs such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc, which support both structural and behavioral modeling. Behavioral dynamics are specified with SysML state‑machine diagrams and MontiArc Automaton extensions. Parametric and simulation models are built in MathWorks Simulink, and translation to executable code is performed in C++ or Java, allowing integration with real‑time controllers. The development environment relies on MBSE tooling that supports multi‑notation model exchange, while simulation engines and code generators provide runtime verification and performance analysis of the twin’s behavior.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: the Digital Twin constellation orchestrates a multi‑notation MBSE environment in which the core functional and logical architecture is modeled in SysML (IBDs, state machines), complemented by component‑connector ADLs such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. Simulation and analytics are performed with MathWorks Simulink, enabling the coupling of data traces with dynamic models. Code generation bridges the high‑level models to executable artefacts in C++ or Java, forming a cohesive, model‑driven pipeline that integrates data intermediaries, simulation engines, and runtime components while preserving a shared architectural view.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models in the Digital Twin constellation are constructed using Model‑Based Systems Engineering practices. SysML v1.7 is employed for structural and behavioural modelling, while architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc are used to define component‑connector topologies. Behavioural dynamics are captured with state‑machine diagrams in SysML or MontiArc Automaton models. For numerical simulation the models are coupled with MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools, and code in C++ or Java can be generated for execution. Data components are managed in model repositories and include contextual data traces aggregated according to the digital shadow reference model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time‑series data from every sensor on the physical robot (e.g., input current, output angle) are streamed in real‑time via MQTT to the digital shadow. The MQTT broker acts as a message bus that ingests raw sensor payloads, which are then aggregated, pre‑processed (e.g., filtering, unit conversion) and abstracted according to the digital shadow reference model, thereby generating contextual data traces for a defined purpose.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The twin’s data transfer relies on MQTT to publish the sensor time‑series from the physical plant to the virtual environment. No additional protocols, topology or encoding specifications are provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and development process follows a four‑step methodology: 1) Problem identification – defining the goal and scope of the twin; 2) Asset and model analysis – inspecting the existing physical asset and identifying relevant subsystem models; 3) Use or creation of calculations or models – deploying or developing simulation models (e.g., SysML, MontiArc/Simulink); 4) Data identification – determining the sensor streams or data sources needed to drive the twin. The process employs Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) practices with SysML to capture requirements, functional and logical architecture, and interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adopts SysML for functional and logical architecture description, and utilizes ACME and AADL as component‑connector ADLs. The digital shadow is formally represented by a UML class diagram as defined in the Digital Shadow reference model. No explicit industry standard compliance, certification framework, or interoperability specification is detailed in the document.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly identifies the system under study. The documents describe the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and specify that the 'first vertical robot axis' is the focus for the digital shadow. It also details the controller used.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes specific services and purposes of the digital shadow. These include 'visualization in a dashboard', 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', and the goal to 'improve the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The extraction is detailed and well-supported by Section II ('Background') of the source text. It correctly identifies Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), SysML, and specific Architecture Description Languages like 'ACME, AADL, and MontiArc'. It also accurately mentions 'MathWorks Simulink' for simulation and 'C++ or Java' for translation. Quotes: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26]', 'MathWorks Simulink [28]', 'code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

The document mentions 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and 'MQTT' for data streaming, but these are used in the context of connecting the specific robot to its digital shadow (asset integration). There is no mention of integration with external enterprise systems, other Digital Twins, or broader interoperability patterns as defined in the characteristic. The extraction correctly identifies this as not in the document.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the document states the goal is to 'improve robot motion accuracy' (Abstract) and 'derive the friction... to improve the robot control’s accuracy' (IV.A), it does not provide details on the types of insights generated (beyond friction coefficients), specific analytics processing, decision-making processes, or user interaction methods with the dashboard beyond general visualization. The text lacks the specific details required by the characteristic description, making 'Not in Document' a fair assessment, although the general goal is mentioned.

The extraction correctly identifies the adoption of SysML for architecture, as seen in Section II.A and Fig 3. It also accurately notes the mention of ACME and AADL as ADLs in the background section ('ACME [23], AADL [24]'). The representation of the digital shadow via a UML class diagram is correctly cited ('Fig. 1: Digital shadow reference model as a UML class diagram'). The statement regarding the lack of explicit industry standard compliance or certification frameworks is accurate, as the text focuses on modeling languages rather than broader industry standards.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source document focuses on the technical implementation of a digital shadow for robot motion control, specifically 'combining analytical (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) friction models'. While it discusses 'traceability' of data origins and sources, it contains no information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection measures, or consent management. Therefore, the extraction stating this is 'Not in Document' is correct.

The document describes a physical 'safety cell' that restricts the robot's working range and mentions 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' as part of the methodology. However, it does not describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or fail-safe mechanisms for the digital system. The text is concerned with data modeling and accuracy, not security architecture. Thus, stating the characteristic is 'Not in Document' is accurate based on the provided definition.
